title: Neural Networks II: Optimizing Training
blurb: ★★★ Core / must-know · Everything that makes training actually work: choosing activation functions, preprocessing data, initializing weights (Xavier/He), Batch Normalization, and searching hyperparameters (trial-and-error, grid, random, Bayesian). Includes numpy code showing how bad initialization causes vanishing/exploding activations, and Keras code for dropout and grid search.

knowledge_points.title: Activation Functions & Weight Initialization
knowledge_points.recap: **Sigmoid** has 3 problems: saturated neurons kill gradients, outputs are not zero-centered, and exp() is expensive. **tanh** is zero-centered but still saturates. **ReLU** is the default: cheap, non-saturating for $x>0$, but not zero-centered and can 'die' for $x<0$. Initialization matters: too-large weights **explode**, too-small **vanish**. **Xavier/Glorot** scales by $1/\sqrt{n_{in}}$ (tanh/sigmoid); **He/Kaiming** scales by $\sqrt{2/n_{in}}$ to compensate for ReLU halving the variance.

questions.q: List the three problems of the **sigmoid** activation and explain why ReLU is preferred in hidden layers.
questions.answer: Sigmoid problems: (1) **saturated neurons kill the gradient** — for large $|x|$ the local gradient is ~0, so backprop stalls; (2) outputs are **not zero-centered**, forcing all-positive or all-negative gradients on the weights (zig-zag updates); (3) **exp() is compute-expensive**. **ReLU** ($\max(0,x)$) does not saturate in the positive region, is very cheap, and converges ~6x faster than sigmoid/tanh, which is why it is the default for hidden layers.
questions.extend: TLDR: use ReLU (mind the learning rate); try Leaky ReLU / ELU / SELU; don't use sigmoid in inner layers.

questions.q: Why do people initialize ReLU neurons with slightly positive biases (e.g. 0.01)?
questions.answer: A ReLU that always receives negative input outputs 0 with zero gradient — a 'dead' neuron that never updates.

options.text: To reduce the chance of 'dead ReLU' neurons that never activate and never update
To make the exp() computation cheaper

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
import numpy as np
x = np.random.randn(512)
for i in range(100):
    w = np.random.randn(512, 512)   # unscaled -> explodes
    x = w @ x
print(np.mean(x), np.std(x))      # huge / NaN

x = np.random.randn(512)
for i in range(100):
    w = np.random.randn(512, 512) * 0.01  # too small -> vanishes
    x = w @ x
print(np.mean(x), np.std(x))      # ~0
```
Unscaled Gaussian weights make activations **explode** across depth; scaling by 0.01 makes them **vanish** to zero. The right scale is in between — this is the **vanishing/exploding gradient** problem that motivates principled initialization.

```python
print(np.____(2)(x), np.____(3)(x))    # report mean / std
```

questions.q: For $y = Wx$ with $x, W \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ and 512 input connections, what is the standard deviation of each output $y_i$? Hence what scale gives unit-variance activations?
questions.answer: Each $y_i = \sum_{k=0}^{511} w_{ik} x_k$ is a sum of 512 unit-variance products, so $\text{Var}(y_i) = 512$ and $\text{std}(y_i) = \sqrt{512} \approx 22.6$. To keep unit variance you scale the weights by $1/\sqrt{512}$ — this is **Xavier/Glorot** initialization.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
import numpy as np
def relu(x): return np.clip(x, 0, None)

x = np.random.randn(512)
for i in range(100):
    w = np.random.randn(512, 512) * (1/np.sqrt(512/2))  # He / Kaiming
    x = relu(w @ x)
print(np.mean(x), np.std(x))   # stays ~unit std
```
Because ReLU zeros out ~half the inputs, it **cuts the variance by a factor of 2**. Scaling weights by $\sqrt{2/n_{in}} = 1/\sqrt{512/2}$ compensates, keeping activation std ~constant through depth. This is **He et al., 2015** initialization.
questions.extend: Xavier uses 1/sqrt(n_in) (good for tanh/sigmoid); He adds the extra /2 factor for ReLU.

```python
for i in range(100):
    w = np.random.randn(512, 512) * (1/np.sqrt(512/____(2)))  # He / Kaiming
    x = relu(w @ x)
```

questions.answer: Xavier for tanh/sigmoid, He for ReLU (extra /2 factor).

options.text: Xavier (1/√n) for tanh/sigmoid; He (√(2/n)) for ReLU
Always initialize all weights to zero
He for tanh; Xavier for ReLU

knowledge_points.title: Data Preprocessing & Batch Normalization
knowledge_points.recap: **Preprocessing**: center the data (zero-mean); for images typically subtract the mean image or per-channel mean. Zero-mean data avoids the all-positive-gradient zig-zag. **Batch Normalization** normalizes each feature to unit Gaussian using minibatch mean/variance, then applies learnable scale $\gamma$ and shift $\beta$. Usually inserted after FC/Conv and before the nonlinearity. It improves gradient flow, allows higher learning rates, reduces dependence on initialization, and acts as light regularization. At **test time** it uses fixed running statistics.

questions.q: Explain what Batch Normalization does, where it is placed, and why the learnable $\gamma$ and $\beta$ parameters are needed.
questions.answer: For a minibatch, BN computes the **empirical mean and variance per dimension** and normalizes each activation to unit Gaussian: $\hat{x} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}}$. It is usually inserted **after a Fully-Connected/Conv layer and before the nonlinearity**. The normalization is then followed by a learnable **scale $\gamma$ and shift $\beta$**: $y = \gamma\hat{x} + \beta$, so the network can undo the normalization (even recover the identity) if strict unit-Gaussian is not desired — e.g. a tanh may not want unit-Gaussian input.
questions.extend: It is a vanilla differentiable function, so it composes cleanly with backprop.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a stated benefit of Batch Normalization?
questions.answer: BN does not eliminate the need for an optimizer; it improves flow, allows higher LR, reduces init dependence, and lightly regularizes.

options.text: It removes the need for an optimizer / gradient descent
Improves gradient flow through the network
Reduces the strong dependence on initialization and acts as light regularization

questions.q: How does a BatchNorm layer behave differently at **test time** versus training time?
questions.answer: At **training** time BN uses the **mean/variance of the current minibatch**. At **test** time the batch statistics are not used; instead a single **fixed empirical mean/variance** (estimated during training via **running averages**) normalizes the activations. This makes test-time output deterministic and independent of batch composition.
questions.extend: This 'add randomness at train, average it out at test' pattern is shared with dropout and data augmentation.

questions.q: Why is zero-mean data preprocessing recommended, and what centering is common for images?
questions.answer: If inputs to a neuron are always positive, the gradients on its weights are **all positive or all negative**, forcing inefficient zig-zag update paths — hence you want **zero-mean** data. For images the common practice is to **center only**: subtract the mean image (e.g. AlexNet) or the **per-channel mean** (e.g. VGGNet). Normalizing variance / PCA / whitening is uncommon for images.

knowledge_points.title: Optimizers, Dropout & Hyperparameter Search (code)
knowledge_points.recap: Build MNIST classifiers and tune them. **Adam** is a strong default optimizer. Fight overfitting from added capacity with **Dropout** (rate ~0.2). Hyperparameter search: **trial-and-error**, **Grid Search** (all combinations, exhaustive/expensive), **Random Search** (sample from ranges, more efficient), and **Bayesian optimization** (uses past results to pick the next guess). Watch the loss curve to diagnose the **learning rate**.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
from tensorflow.keras import models, layers

model = models.Sequential()
model.add(layers.Dense(50, input_shape=(784,), activation='relu'))
model.add(layers.Dropout(0.2))
for _ in range(4):
    model.add(layers.Dense(50, activation='relu'))
    model.add(layers.Dropout(0.2))
model.add(layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax'))

model.compile(loss='categorical_crossentropy',
              optimizer='adam', metrics=['accuracy'])
```
**Dropout** randomly zeros 20% of each layer's activations during training to reduce overfitting; **softmax + categorical_crossentropy** is the multi-class pairing; **Adam** is the default optimizer.
questions.extend: Dropout only helps once you have enough capacity/epochs — the lab needed ~50 epochs before the dropout net improved.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
from scikeras.wrappers import KerasClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

def create_model(neurons=50):
    model = models.Sequential()
    model.add(layers.Dense(neurons, input_shape=(784,), activation='relu'))
    model.add(layers.Dropout(0.2))
    model.add(layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax'))
    model.compile(loss='categorical_crossentropy',
                  optimizer='adam', metrics=['accuracy'])
    return model

clf = KerasClassifier(build_fn=create_model, epochs=5, batch_size=32, neurons=50)
params = dict(neurons=[50, 100, 250, 500])
grid = GridSearchCV(estimator=clf, param_grid=params)
grid_result = grid.fit(train_images, train_labels)
print(grid_result.best_score_, grid_result.best_params_)
```
Grid search trains a model for **every** combination in the grid — thorough but expensive.
questions.extend: RandomizedSearchCV(..., param_distributions=params, n_iter=2) samples combinations instead of trying all.

questions.q: Why is **Random Search** often more efficient than **Grid Search** for hyperparameter tuning?
questions.answer: With the same budget, random search tries more distinct values per parameter, so it explores important dimensions better.

options.text: For the same number of trials it tries more distinct values per hyperparameter, so it finds good regions faster (especially when some parameters matter more)
It guarantees trying every combination in the grid
It never needs a validation set
It always uses fewer parameters than grid search

questions.q: Describe how the **loss curve** tells you your learning rate is too high or too low.
questions.answer: **Too low**: the loss barely changes / decreases very slowly (nearly flat curve). **Too high**: the loss **explodes** — it goes up or returns **NaN** (cost > ~3x original is a red flag). A curve that decreases steadily then plateaus is healthy. The lab's rule of thumb: cross-validate the learning rate roughly in $[10^{-3}, 10^{-5}]$, and search in **log space**.
questions.extend: A loss that stays flat then suddenly drops often points to bad initialization.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
import torch, torch.nn as nn

model = MyMLP()
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()          # includes softmax
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)

for xb, yb in loader:
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    logits = model(xb)
    loss = criterion(logits, yb)
    loss.backward()      # compute gradients
    optimizer.step()     # update weights
```
`nn.CrossEntropyLoss` expects **raw logits** (softmax is applied internally). The three-line `zero_grad -> backward -> step` is the PyTorch training loop core.

```python
import tensorflow as tf

from tensorflow.keras.callbacks import EarlyStopping
```

knowledge_points.title: 🔮 SS26 Likely Exam Questions (predicted)
knowledge_points.recap: Predicted, exam-style questions in the professor's format — concept-first, plus a Keras code item where relevant. Not from a past paper; use to rehearse likely SS26 topics.

questions.q: (a) Why is **weight initialization** important — what goes wrong if the weights are too large or too small? (b) Which initialization scheme would you **use** for a deep **ReLU** network, and which one would you **not** use, and why?
questions.answer:
**(a)** In a deep net the activation variance compounds layer by layer. **Too-large** weights make activations (and gradients) **explode** across depth; **too-small** weights make them **vanish** toward zero. Either way the gradients become useless and training stalls — the **vanishing/exploding gradient** problem. Good initialization keeps the activation variance roughly **constant through depth**.

**(b)** For ReLU, **use He / Kaiming** initialization, which scales weights by $\sqrt{2/n_{in}}$. The **factor of 2** compensates for ReLU zeroing out about half its inputs and thus halving the variance. I would **not** use plain **Xavier/Glorot** ($1/\sqrt{n_{in}}$) for a ReLU net: it is derived for zero-centered activations like **tanh/sigmoid** and, lacking the $\times 2$ correction, lets the activations gradually **shrink** through the ReLU layers. (Initializing all weights to **zero** is also wrong — every neuron would stay identical.)
questions.extend: Bad init ⇒ vanishing/exploding activations. ReLU → He ($\sqrt{2/n}$); tanh/sigmoid → Xavier ($1/\sqrt{n}$); never all-zeros.

questions.q: (a) What does **Batch Normalization** do and where is it placed in the network? (b) What benefits does it offer during training? (c) How does its behaviour differ at **test time** versus training time?
questions.answer:
**(a)** For each minibatch, BN computes the **per-dimension mean and variance** and normalizes each activation to unit Gaussian, $\hat x=\frac{x-\mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2+\epsilon}}$, then applies a **learnable scale $\gamma$ and shift $\beta$**: $y=\gamma\hat x+\beta$ (so the network can undo the normalization if needed). It is usually inserted **after a Fully-Connected/Conv layer and before the non-linearity**.

**(b)** It **improves gradient flow**, **allows higher learning rates**, **reduces the strong dependence on initialization**, and acts as **light regularization**. (It does **not** remove the need for an optimizer.)

**(c)** At **training** time BN uses the **current minibatch's** statistics. At **test** time it instead uses **fixed running-average** mean/variance estimated during training, so the output is **deterministic** and independent of batch composition.
questions.extend: BN: normalize per-dim then γ,β; after FC/Conv, before non-linearity. Benefits: flow, higher LR, less init-sensitive, light reg. Test time = fixed running stats.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras import models, layers
from tensorflow.keras.callbacks import EarlyStopping

model = models.Sequential()
# hidden layers: ReLU + L2 weight penalty, Dropout after each
model.add(layers.Dense(256, activation='relu', input_shape=(784,),
                       kernel_regularizer=tf.keras.regularizers.L2(0.001)))
model.add(layers.Dropout(0.3))
model.add(layers.Dense(256, activation='relu',
                       kernel_regularizer=tf.keras.regularizers.L2(0.001)))
model.add(layers.Dropout(0.3))
model.add(layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax'))   # 10-class output

model.compile(optimizer='adam',
              loss='categorical_crossentropy',
              metrics=['accuracy'])

es = EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss', patience=3, restore_best_weights=True)
history = model.fit(x_train, y_train,
                    epochs=50, batch_size=128,
                    validation_data=(x_val, y_val),
                    callbacks=[es])

test_loss, test_acc = model.evaluate(x_test, y_test)
```
The three anti-overfitting tools are all here: **L2** (`kernel_regularizer`) penalizes large weights, **Dropout(0.3)** randomly zeros units during training, and **EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss')** halts once validation loss stops improving. Note Dropout only helps once the model has enough capacity/epochs.
questions.extend: Overfitting toolkit = L2 (kernel_regularizer) + Dropout + EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss'); keep softmax + categorical_crossentropy + validation_data + evaluate.
